title: Embeddings & Vector Databases
description: Embeddings, similarity search, ANN indexes, hybrid retrieval, metadata filtering, and vector database tradeoffs.

questions.title: What are embeddings and why are they useful?
questions.scenario: You need users to find policy documents even when they use different wording than the document.
questions.expectedAnswer: Embeddings are numeric vector representations that place semantically related items close together in vector space. They enable semantic search, clustering, recommendations, deduplication, and retrieval for RAG. A good answer mentions that embedding quality depends on model choice, domain fit, normalization, and evaluation.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Embeddings & Vector Databases?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise embeddings & vector databases setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Compare cosine similarity, dot product, and Euclidean distance.
questions.scenario: Search quality drops after switching embedding providers without changing index settings.
questions.expectedAnswer: Cosine similarity compares vector direction and is common for normalized embeddings. Dot product is sensitive to both direction and magnitude and is often used by retrieval models trained for it. Euclidean distance measures straight-line distance and can be sensitive to magnitude. The right metric should match the embedding model and index configuration.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.

questions.title: What is approximate nearest neighbor search and why is it used?
questions.scenario: A product search system must query 100 million vectors under tight latency targets.
questions.expectedAnswer: ANN search trades perfect exhaustive search for speed and scale. Indexes like HNSW, IVF, and DiskANN retrieve likely nearest vectors efficiently across millions or billions of embeddings. Candidates should discuss recall-latency tradeoffs, index build cost, memory, filtering, and evaluation against exact search samples.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: How should metadata filtering work in vector search?
questions.scenario: An enterprise assistant must retrieve only documents the current user can access.
questions.expectedAnswer: Metadata filtering constrains retrieval by fields such as tenant, document type, language, freshness, source, or access control. It can happen before or during ANN search depending on the database. Strong answers mention ACL filtering, filter selectivity, index design, and avoiding post-generation permission checks.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: When is hybrid search better than pure vector search?
questions.scenario: Users search for product SKUs, policy names, and vague semantic questions in the same system.
questions.expectedAnswer: Hybrid search combines dense semantic retrieval with sparse lexical methods like BM25. It helps when exact terms, IDs, error codes, names, or rare domain phrases matter. A strong answer explains score fusion, reranking, query intent, and evaluation instead of assuming one retrieval method always wins.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.

questions.title: How would you explain embedding model selection in a Embeddings & Vector Databases interview?
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through embedding model selection for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define embedding model selection, explain why it matters for embeddings & vector databases, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations.
